title: Configuration Change Audit Trail

compliance.assurance_rationale: Records configuration, provisioning, and template-related changes made through Catalyst Center, supporting change control evidence.
compliance.controlObjective: Helps ensure configuration changes to managed infrastructure are visible and attributable, aligning with NIST 800-53 CM-3 (configuration change control) for the operations plane.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search `UC-5.13.46` with export or dashboard snapshot tied to a change record when reviewing CM-3 effectiveness.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Exposes who initiated configuration and provisioning work on in-scope network equipment for reconciliation with approved change records.
compliance.controlObjective: Supports matching Catalyst Center-originated changes to authorized change management tickets for financial-reporting–relevant network controls.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Monthly CSV or scheduled PDF from `UC-5.13.46` cross-walked to the change-ticket list in the SOX control sample.

dataSources: `index=catalyst sourcetype=cisco:dnac:audit:logs`. from the `audit_logs` modular input in the Cisco Catalyst Add-on (Splunkbase 7538) polling `GET /dna/data/api/v1/event/event-series/audit-log`. Default poll interval: 300s (5m). Volume: Variable; ~10-100 events/day in typical environments. RBAC: SUPER-ADMIN-ROLE. Key fields: `auditUserName` (string), `auditRequestType` (string), `auditDescription` (string), `auditParentId` (string), `createdDateTime` (epoch milliseconds), `isSystemEvent` (boolean). CIM data model: Change.
spl: index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs" (auditRequestType="CONFIG*" OR auditRequestType="*PROVISION*" OR auditRequestType="*TEMPLATE*") | stats count as change_count values(auditDescription) as actions by auditUserName, auditIpAddress | sort -change_count
description: Tracks all configuration changes made through Catalyst Center, including template deployments, provisioning actions, and policy changes.
value: Configuration changes are the leading cause of network outages. A complete audit trail enables rapid rollback and root-cause identification.
implementation: Enable the `audit_logs` input in the Cisco Catalyst TA pointing to `index=catalyst`. The TA polls audit log data from the Catalyst Center Intent API `/dna/intent/api/v1/audit/logs` every 5 minutes. Key fields: `auditRequestType`, `auditDescription`, `auditUserName`, `auditTimestamp`, `auditIpAddress`.
visualization: Table (auditUserName, auditIpAddress, change_count, actions), timechart of config-related events, drilldown to raw events for ticket correlation.

references.title: Catalyst Center Audit Log API — Cisco DevNet

knownFalsePositives:
**Scheduled template synchronization generating regular configuration change entries.** Catalyst Center periodically synchronizes device configurations with golden templates, generating audit log entries for each sync even when no actual change occurs. Distinguish by checking whether the audit entry's `auditDescription` indicates a scheduled sync rather than a manual configuration push. Suppress by filtering scheduled sync entries from the change trail using a `catalyst_automation_patterns` lookup.

**Plug and Play (PnP) onboarding new devices generating configuration change events.** When new devices are onboarded via PnP, their initial configuration generates multiple audit entries. These are expected as part of the provisioning workflow. Distinguish by correlating with `index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs" auditResourceType="PNP"` to identify PnP-related changes. Suppress by categorizing PnP changes separately from ad-hoc configuration modifications.

**SD-Access fabric provisioning generating bulk configuration changes.** When a new fabric site or virtual network is provisioned, Catalyst Center pushes configurations to multiple devices simultaneously, generating a burst of change audit entries. Distinguish by checking whether the changes are clustered in time and affect devices within the same `siteId` or fabric domain. Suppress by requiring changes to be from non-automated `username` values for the ad-hoc change alert.

**Configuration rollback audit entries appearing as new changes.** When a configuration is rolled back to a previous version, the rollback itself generates audit entries that may appear as new changes. Distinguish by checking whether the `auditDescription` contains rollback-related keywords. Do not suppress — rollbacks are important changes that should be tracked, but they should be tagged as rollbacks rather than forward changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cisco Catalyst Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 7538) ≥1.0 with the **audit_logs** modular input enabled, writing to `index=catalyst` with `sourcetype=cisco:dnac:audit:logs`.
• Catalyst Center **2.3.5+** with audit logging enabled (enabled by default; verify under System > Settings > Audit Log).
• RBAC: service account with **SUPER-ADMIN-ROLE** — audit log API access requires elevated privileges.
• Baseline knowledge: understand the types of configuration changes expected in your environment (scheduled template syncs, PnP provisioning, SD-Access fabric operations) to distinguish routine automation from ad-hoc manual changes.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
The TA polls `GET /dna/data/api/v1/event/event-series/audit-log` at the configured interval (default 300-900 seconds). Each event represents one audit log entry from Catalyst Center.

Key fields for configuration change tracking:
• `auditResourceType`: the type of resource that was changed (e.g., "TEMPLATE", "DEVICE_CONFIG", "NETWORK_PROFILE", "SITE", "CREDENTIAL").
• `auditDescription`: human-readable description of the change (e.g., "Template deployed to device", "Network Profile updated").
• `username`: the administrator or service account that performed the change.
• `auditRequestType`: HTTP method indicating the type of operation — POST (create), PUT (update), DELETE (remove), GET (read — usually excluded from change tracking).
• `auditParentId`: groups related audit entries from the same workflow (e.g., a template deployment generates multiple entries).
• `isSystemEvent`: boolean — true for Catalyst Center internal operations, false for administrator actions.
• `createdAt`: timestamp of the audit entry (use for time-based analysis).

Expected volume: 50-500 audit entries/day in a moderately active environment; spikes to 1,000+ during template deployments or SD-Access provisioning.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs" auditRequestType IN ("POST","PUT","DELETE") isSystemEvent!=true
| stats count as change_count values(auditDescription) as changes dc(auditParentId) as workflow_count by username, auditResourceType
| sort -change_count
```

Understanding this SPL:
• **`auditRequestType IN ("POST","PUT","DELETE")`**: Filters to state-changing operations only. GET requests are read operations that do not modify the system.
• **`isSystemEvent!=true`**: Excludes Catalyst Center internal system operations (health checks, scheduled scans), focusing on administrator-initiated changes.
• **`dc(auditParentId) as workflow_count`**: Counts distinct workflows rather than individual audit entries. A single template deployment may generate 10+ audit entries, but they all share the same `auditParentId`.
• **`by username, auditResourceType`**: Groups changes by who made them and what type of resource was changed, enabling quick identification of unexpected changes.

For timeline analysis: `| timechart span=1h count by auditResourceType` — reveals patterns in configuration change activity.

Step 3 — Validate
• **UI comparison:** Open Catalyst Center > System > Audit Logs. Filter by time range and compare the total count and change types with the Splunk results.
• **Change verification:** Make a known configuration change in Catalyst Center (e.g., rename a site, update a template). Verify the audit entry appears in Splunk within 2 poll cycles with the correct `username`, `auditResourceType`, and `auditDescription`.
• **Count check:** Compare `| stats count` in Splunk for a 24-hour period with the audit log entry count in the Catalyst Center UI. Allow ±5% variance due to poll timing.
• **Vendor UI parity:** cross-reference the Splunk results with the corresponding **Catalyst Center > Assurance** page for the same time window to confirm counts and values match.
Step 4 — Operationalize
• **Dashboard:** Configuration change timeline (timechart by `auditResourceType`), top changers table (by `username`), and recent critical changes table filtered to TEMPLATE, DEVICE_CONFIG, and CREDENTIAL resource types.
• **Alert:** Trigger on high-impact change types: `auditResourceType IN ("CREDENTIAL","NETWORK_PROFILE","TEMPLATE") AND auditRequestType="DELETE"` — credential deletion or template removal requires immediate attention.
• **SIEM correlation:** Forward configuration change events to the SIEM for correlation with security events (e.g., credential change followed by unauthorized access).

Step 5 — Troubleshoot
• **No audit entries:** Verify the `audit_logs` input is enabled in the TA. Check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ExecProcessor "TA_cisco_catalyst"` for API errors. The audit log API requires SUPER-ADMIN-ROLE.
• **Missing recent changes:** The TA polls at intervals; changes made between polls will appear in the next poll cycle. Reduce the poll interval if near-real-time audit tracking is required.
• **System events overwhelming the results:** Add `| where isSystemEvent!=true` to all audit log dashboards. System events are useful for platform health monitoring (UC-5.13.45) but create noise in change tracking.
• **auditParentId grouping incomplete:** Some workflows span multiple API calls with different `auditParentId` values. Use `| transaction username maxpause=5m` as an alternative grouping strategy.
grandmaExplanation: We record every configuration change made to your network through Catalyst Center, so you always know who changed what and when.
